data.title: Donegal Group Inc. Reports Fourth Quarter and Full Year 2025 Results
data.description:
Donegal Group Inc., an insurance holding company, has released its financial results for the fourth quarter and full year ending December 31, 2025. The company operates through its subsidiaries and affiliates, offering property and casualty insurance across various states in the Mid-Atlantic, Southern, Midwestern, and Southwestern regions.

Financial Highlights

For the fourth quarter of 2025, Donegal Group Inc. reported a net income of $17.2 million, or 47 cents per diluted Class A share, a decrease from $24.0 million, or 70 cents per diluted Class A share, in the same period of 2024. Net premiums earned decreased by 4.1% to $226.9 million, and the combined ratio increased to 96.3% from 92.9% in the previous year. The full year results showed a net income of $79.3 million, or $2.18 per diluted Class A share, up from $50.9 million, or $1.53 per diluted Class A share, in 2024. Net premiums earned for the full year decreased by 1.7% to $921.2 million, while the combined ratio improved to 95.4% from 98.6%.

Business and Operational Highlights

In the commercial lines segment, net premiums earned increased by 3.0% for both the fourth quarter and the full year of 2025. However, the personal lines segment saw a decline, with net premiums earned decreasing by 13.9% for the fourth quarter and 8.0% for the full year. The company attributed the decrease in personal lines to planned attrition and strategic non-renewal actions.

Strategic Initiatives and Corporate Developments

Donegal Group Inc. has focused on strategic initiatives to drive growth and profitability. The company has engaged its agents in developing detailed growth plans and introduced new compensation incentives to encourage increased submissions of new quality accounts. Additionally, the company is making progress on converting legacy policies to a new platform, expected to be completed by June 2027.

Management's Perspective

Kevin G. Burke, President and CEO, expressed satisfaction with the company's solid operating performance, attributing it to strategic decisions and actions taken over the past several years. He emphasized the company's focus on modest premium growth through independent agency partners and the commitment to capitalizing on opportunities for profitable growth.

Future Outlook

Looking ahead, Donegal Group Inc. aims to achieve modest premium growth in 2026 through its independent agency partners. The company plans to maintain profitability in its personal lines segment while increasing new business success rates. The expense ratio impact of allocated costs related to the systems modernization project is expected to be 1.3 percentage points for the full year of 2026, subsiding gradually over the next several years.

Original SEC Filing: DONEGAL GROUP INC [ DGICA ] - 8-K - Feb. 19, 2026

Disclaimer
This is an AI-powered summary. It may contain inaccuracies. Consider verifying important information with the source. Please note this summary is solely based on documents filed with the SEC.

data.title: ROUNDUP: EPAM Systems Sees Strong Growth In Q1, FY26, But Shares Down 3.4%
data.description:
WASHINGTON (dpa-AFX) - While reporting financial results for the fourth quarter on Thursday, digital engineering company EPAM Systems, Inc. (EPAM) provided earnings, adjusted earnings and revenue guidance for the first quarter and for the full-year 2026.

For the first quarter, the company expects earnings in a range of $1.32 to $1.40 per share and adjusted earnings in a range of $2.70 to $2.78 per share on revenues between $1.385 billion and $1.40 billion.

Looking ahead to fiscal 2026, the company now projects earnings in a range of $7.95 to $8.25 per share and adjusted earnings in a range of $12.60 to $12.90 per share on revenue growth of 4.5 to 7.5 percent, with organic constant currency growth of 3 to 6 percent.

In Thursday's pre-market trading, EPAM is trading on the NYSE at $161.80, up $5.74 or 3.42 percent.

data.title: UK Manufacturing Orders Remain Weak: CBI
data.description:
The UK’s total order book balance rose to -28 in February 2026 from -30 in January, according to the Confederation of British Industry (CBI).

Although the reading signals that industrial orders are declining at their slowest pace since September, levels remain historically subdued.

Export order books were also reported as below “normal,” though slightly improved at -26 compared with -30 in January.

In the three months to February, manufacturing output continued to fall but at a slower pace than in the three months to January (-14 vs -25).

Expectations for average selling price inflation remained elevated at +26% in February, easing slightly from +29% in January but still well above the long-run average of +8%.

CBI Senior Economist Cameron Martin said the downturn in manufacturing output had moderated in February following a difficult start to the year, though many firms continue to report customers holding back amid weak confidence and persistent cost pressures.

data.title: YETI Holdings Names Scott Bomar Chief Financial Officer
data.description:
By Colin Kellaher

YETI Holdings is replacing its top finance executive.

YETI on Thursday said it has Scott Bomar as the coolers and insulated drinkware maker's new senior vice president, chief financial officer and treasurer, effective Feb. 23.

YETI said it has informed Mike McMullen, who has been finance chief since February 2023, that his role would end on that date.

Bomar, 54 years old, joins YETI from Home Depot, where he has been senior vice president of finance since 2022.

YETI said Bomar will receive an annual base salary of $725,000, a $500,000 sign-on bonus and an annual bonus with a target of 100% of base pay.

YETI said McMullen, who joined the company in 2016, will stay on in an advisory capacity until the end of May, adding that he will be entitled to severance benefits.

Write to Colin Kellaher at colin.kellaher@wsj.com

data.title: Besi Shares Fall as Key Orders Metric Disappoints
data.description:
By Joe Stonor

Shares in BE Semiconductor Industries fell after demand for the semiconductor company's flagship hybrid bonding technology came in lower than investors hoped.

The Amsterdam-listed semiconductor packaging company fell 7%, or 13.05 euros, to 173.9 euros in early afternoon European trade, putting it among the sharpest fallers in the Europe-wide Stoxx 600 index.

Revenue slipped 2.7% in 2025 to 591.3 million euros ($696.70 million), despite a 25% revenue jump in the fourth quarter. Gross margins slid from 65.3% to 63.3% on-year, while earnings per share fell 28.1% to 1.66 euros.

The company booked orders worth 695 million euros in 2025, a near-17% rise compared to the previous year. Orders surged 43% into the fourth quarter, boosted by an uptick in demand from Chinese customers.

Besi management flagged growth in AI-driven demand for ultra-advanced hybrid bonding, as well as a pickup in mainstream semiconductor customers like industrial and automotive companies.

"Green shoots are appearing after an extended downturn of nearly four years in each of our principal end-user markets," Besi Chief Executive Richard Blickman said.

However, the advanced hybrid bonding specialist hasn't grown orders as quickly as some of its peers. Though the number of customers using the company's flagship hybrid bonding technology grew to 18--up from 15 at the end of 2024--the increase is lower than investors hoped.

At the mid-point of Besi guidance, the company expects 300 orders for the technology at the end of this year. Orders grew to just around 150 at the end of 2025, making the prospect of reaching 300 more uncertain, analysts at ING noted.

Besi shares have surged in recent months, as the company and its European peers including ASML and ASM International look to benefit from AI-fueled chip demand. The stock is up around 30% so far this year despite Thursday's fall.

Write to Joe Stonor at josephmichael.stonor@wsj.com

data.title: US Border Enforcement At Historic Levels
data.description:
WASHINGTON (dpa-AFX) - U.S. Customs and Border Protection has released operational statistics for January under the Trump administration's border security policies, which shows border enforcement reaching historic levels.

'For the ninth consecutive month, CBP has delivered historic results-Border Patrol apprehensions are down 96% from the previous administration's monthly average,' said CBP Commissioner Rodney S. Scott. 'This historic reduction reflects the dedication of our agents and officers to securing our borders, protecting our communities, and upholding the rule of law.'

The Office of Field Operations and U.S. Border Patrol together have carried out 34,626 encounters nationwide. It is 91 percent below the peak of the Biden administration's 370,883.

Border Patrol agents made 6,070 apprehensions on the southwest border, which is 96 percent lower than the monthly average of the previous administration, and less than what was apprehended in six days in January 2025.

196 USBP apprehensions per day on the southwest border is 96 percent lower than the daily average under the previous administration.

No one was released on parole for the ninth consecutive month, CBP said in a press release.

In January 2026, CBP encountered 34,626 illegal aliens crossing U.S. borders at and between ports of entry nationwide. It marks a 13 percent increase from December 2025, but a 58 percent decrease from January 2025. Nationwide encounters averaged 1,117 per day, 58 percent lower than the 2,628 daily average from January 2025.

Along the southwest border, CBP encountered 9,726 illegal aliens, an 84 percent decrease from January 2025. Border Patrol apprehended 6,070 illegal aliens on the southwest border, a 79 percent decrease from January 2025.

CBP also provided data on its fight against drug smuggling, seizing dangerous narcotics before they reach American communities.

In January, CBP seized 785 pounds of fentanyl.

Combined seizures of cocaine, methamphetamine, heroin, fentanyl, and marijuana increased 15 percent from December.

CBP, which plays a central role in enforcing U.S. trade law and supporting economic security, said it processed $267 billion in imports in January, and identified $27.8 billion in duties for collection.

From January 20, 2025, through January 31, 2026, CBP assessed $253.3 billion from all tariffs, taxes, and fees.

CBP also detailed efforts targeting forced labor and counterfeit goods.

In January, CBP stopped 530 shipments valued at more than $29.94 million for potential forced labor violations, and seized 2,190 shipments containing counterfeit goods valued at more than $355.7 million.

Copyright(c) 2026 RTTNews.com. All Rights Reserved

Copyright RTT News/dpa-AFX

data.title: Time to Lock In Gains in Ten-Year Gilt Yields — Market Talk
data.description: The market positioning in 10-year U.K. government bonds, or gilts, appears overdone and investors are likely better off locking in recent gains in that segment, TD Securities Pooja Kumra says in a note. Ten-year gilts have performed well in recent months, with yields falling and prices rising, as markets price in higher prospects of interest-rate cuts by the Bank of England. Yields are projected to fall further to around 4.10% by the end of 2026, but taking profits at current levels looks favorable, Kumra says. Ten-year gilt yields last trade at 4.394%, Tradeweb data show. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

data.title: Forward prices rise on support from fuel markets
data.description:
Nordic power prices rose on Thursday, as support from fuel markets due to supply disruption concerns arising from U.S.-Iran tensions lifted the market.

* Nordic front-quarter baseload power contract (ENOFBLQc1) rose 0.8 euros or 1.7% to 47.8 euros per megawatt hour (MWh) at 1030 GMT

Nordic front-year contract (ENOFBLYc1) gained 0.53 euros or 1.27% to 42.1 euros per megawatt hour (MWh)

* "I believe bullish gas, coal and carbon prices, due to the increased tensions between the U.S. and Iran, are the main reasons for today's price rise," said Oletom Djupskaas, a power analyst at LSEG.

* Dutch and British wholesale gas prices rose sharply on Thursday, buoyed by tensions between the U.S. and Iran that could impact the shipping of liquefied natural gas.

* Iran issued a notice to airmen (NOTAM) that it plans rocket launches in areas across its south on Thursday, while the U.S. has deployed warships near Iran. The U.S. said on Wednesday that the two sides were still far apart on some issues after U.S.-Iran talks in Geneva on Tuesday.

* "A much lower-than-expected spot price for Thursday and/or a bearish change to fuel prices could turn the market bearish, however, I don't view this as very likely," Djupskaas added.

* The Nordic power price for next-day physical delivery (FXSYSAL=NPX), or system price, fell 6.2 euros or 4.81% to 122.71 euros per megawatt hour (MWh)

* "Next week will see generally increasing weather activity in the southern parts with near-normal temperatures and partially above-normal precipitation," LSEG meteorologist Georg Mueller noted.

* Nordic water reserves available 15 days ahead (PCAEC00) were at 28.03 terawatt hours below normal, compared with 28.15 terawatt hours below normal on Wednesday.

data.title: Sam Altman Urges Caution On Superintelligent AI – Says Biggest Risks Remain Unknown
data.description:
OpenAI CEO Sam Altman urged policymakers and technologists to approach the rise of superintelligent artificial intelligence with caution, warning that major unknowns remain about how such systems could reshape governments and global security.

Speaking at India’s AI summit, Altman acknowledged growing public pressure for clarity about advanced AI but stressed restraint, emphasizing that even industry leaders lack definitive answers about the technology’s long-term trajectory.

Call For Caution

Altman told attendees that society must accept uncertainty as AI capabilities accelerate. 

“We understand that with technology this powerful, people want answers. But it's important to be humble about what we don't know and always remember that sometimes our best guess is wrong.” 

-San Altman, CEO, OpenAI

Get updates to this developing story directly on Stocktwits.

data.title: Diesel market backwardation steepens; cash diffs hold steady
data.description:
Asia's diesel market structure went into a steeper backwardation, supported by ICE gasoil futures gains, though window spot activity was slightly thin.

Support has mostly come from geopolitical drivers in recent weeks, several trade sources say, with expectations of firmer supply-demand fundamentals taking a backseat driver for now.

Meanwhile, traders were eyeing March export estimate values from China to gauge the market's supply balance, with expectations of these volumes to be elevated these two months.

Also, some refiners were likely to emerge in the next few trading sessions to continue their March spot sales after the holidays.

Refining margins (GO10SGCKMc1) gained to around $20.7 a barrel, erasing the prior sessions' softness.

The 10ppm sulphur gasoil cash differentials (GO10-SIN-DIF) barely moved, hanging on at $1.37 a barrel premium, reflecting a lower-priced offer on the trading window.

For jet fuel, the arbitrage spread between Asia and the U.S. west coast widened since late last week amid the jump in U.S. west coast prices overnight.

Some traders were still expecting jet fuel market strength to emerge soon because of healthier demand expectations next month from a handful of regional importers.

Regrade (JETREG10SGMc1) discounts narrowed slightly to around 80 cents per barrel.

SINGAPORE CASH DEALS

- No deal for jet fuel or gasoil

INVENTORIES

- U.S. crude and gasoline stocks and distillate inventories fell last week, market sources said, citing American Petroleum Institute figures on Wednesday.

- Middle distillates stocks held at Fujairah Oil Industry Zone fell slightly to a two-week low of around 3.03 million barrels in the week of February 16, according to industry information service S&P Global Commodity Insights.

NEWS

- Global oil firms and traders including Vitol, Trafigura and TotalEnergies have won tenders to supply Libya with gasoline and diesel as the country grants large Western players wider access and reduces imports of Russian fuel, three trading sources told Reuters.

- Glencore traded more oil last year but earned less from its energy trading business for the third year in a row, the commodity trader and mining group's annual earnings showed on Wednesday.

- Russian crude shipments in January made up the smallest portion of India's oil imports since late 2022, according to data from industry sources, while Middle Eastern supplies rose to their highest share over the same period.

- Oil prices rose on Thursday as the U.S. and Iran attempted to ease a standoff in talks over Tehran's nuclear programme while both sides heightened military activity in the key oil-producing region.

data.title: Deere Profit Sinks as Tariffs Weigh on Margins
data.description:
By Nicholas G. Miller

Deere posted lower first-quarter profit, which was dragged down by tariffs and weak tractor demand.

The company posted net income of $656 million, or $2.42 a share, down from $869 million, or $3.19 a share, the year before. Analysts polled by FactSet had expected $2.02 a share.

Net sales and revenue rose 13% to $9.61 billion, boosted by a 34% increase in net sales for its construction and forestry division and 24% net sales growth for its small agriculture and turf segment.

The company raised its fiscal-year net income guidance to $4.5 billion to $5 billion, up from its previous forecast of $4 billion to $4.75 billion. Analysts see $4.43 billion.

"While the global large agriculture industry continues to experience challenges, we're encouraged by the ongoing recovery in demand within both the construction and small agriculture segments," said Chief Executive John May.

Write to Nicholas G. Miller at nicholas.miller@wsj.com
